title: GoHighLevel Pricing 2026: $97 vs $297 vs $497, What You Really Pay
desc: GoHighLevel pricing explained for 2026: Starter $97, Unlimited $297, Agency Pro $497, plus the usage fees, AI costs, and add-ons that decide your real monthly bill.
keywords: gohighlevel pricing, gohighlevel pricing 2026, gohighlevel plans, how much does gohighlevel cost, gohighlevel hidden costs, gohighlevel ai pricing, gohighlevel $97 vs $297
cta: Want a GoHighLevel build that pays for itself?
body:
Quick answer: GoHighLevel has three plans in 2026: Starter at $97 per month, Unlimited at $297 per month, and Agency Pro at $497 per month. Most agencies should start on Unlimited at $297, because it unlocks unlimited client sub-accounts, API access, and at-cost rebilling. A solo operator or a single business does fine on Starter at $97. Choose Agency Pro at $497 only when you are ready to resell the platform under your own brand with SaaS Mode. Whatever plan you pick, budget for usage on top: email at $0.675 per 1,000 sends, SMS at roughly $0.0079 per segment in the US, and AI that is either pay per use or $97 per month unlimited per sub-account. For most users the extras land between $20 and $150 per month.

Those base prices have held steady, but HighLevel moved several AI features to new pricing in 2026 and quietly expanded what the usage wallet covers. This guide breaks down every number from HighLevel's own pricing and billing documentation, current as of June 2026, so you can predict your real bill before you commit. For the full feature walkthrough, read our honest GoHighLevel review for 2026.

GoHighLevel pricing at a glance

  • Starter, $97 per month: the full CRM, funnel and website builder, automations, calendars, reputation management, courses, unlimited contacts and unlimited users, with up to 3 sub-accounts.
  • Unlimited, $297 per month: everything in Starter plus unlimited sub-accounts, API access, a branded desktop app, and rebilling of usage to clients at cost.
  • Agency Pro, $497 per month: everything in Unlimited plus SaaS Mode, rebilling with your own markup, and advanced API access. This is the plan for selling GoHighLevel as your own software.
  • Annual billing: saves about 17 percent, roughly two months free. Starter works out near $81 per month, Unlimited near $248, Agency Pro near $414.
  • Free trial: 14 days on every plan, card required.

Every tier includes unlimited contacts and users, so the subscription itself will not surprise you. The surprises live in the usage wallet and the add-ons, which is where the rest of this guide goes.

Starter at $97: right for one business, tight for an agency

The $97 plan is a real version of the platform, not a teaser. You get the same CRM, funnels, email, SMS, automations, and booking tools as the bigger plans. The cap that matters is 3 sub-accounts. A sub-account is one business workspace, so Starter suits a single company running its own marketing, a freelancer, or a brand new agency with one or two clients.

What Starter lacks is API access and rebilling. Without rebilling, every text your clients send comes out of your wallet with no clean way to charge it back. That is fine at two clients and painful at five. Most agencies outgrow Starter within months, which HighLevel surely knows.

Unlimited at $297: the agency default

The $297 plan is where GoHighLevel makes economic sense for agencies. Unlimited sub-accounts means client number twelve costs you the same subscription as client number three, which is the whole pitch: your software cost stays flat while your retainers stack. API access opens custom integrations, and at-cost rebilling lets each client sub-account pay for its own SMS, email, and AI usage instead of you absorbing it.

The break-even math is short. Manage two or three clients at even a modest retainer and the plan covers itself, especially once it replaces separate subscriptions for funnels, email, scheduling, and call tracking. One honest warning: the platform is broad, and the learning curve is real. Budget two to four weeks to get fluent, or have a team like our GoHighLevel services crew configure it for you.

Start your 14-day GoHighLevel free trial

Agency Pro at $497: SaaS Mode and markup money

The $497 plan exists for one model: selling GoHighLevel as your own branded software. SaaS Mode lets you define your own plans, prices, and feature sets, then onboard customers who pay you monthly for what is, underneath, a white-labeled GoHighLevel. Two capabilities make the price back. First, rebilling with markup: you can resell SMS, email, phone, and AI usage at a margin you set, so usage becomes a profit line instead of a cost line. Second, the SaaS Configurator automates signup, billing through Stripe, and sub-account creation.

Ten SaaS customers at $197 per month is $1,970 of recurring revenue against a $497 platform bill plus usage you are reselling at markup anyway. That is why agencies that commit to the SaaS model rarely complain about this plan's price. If you are not reselling, stay on Unlimited; you would be paying $200 extra for features you will not use.

The usage costs nobody puts on the pricing page

GoHighLevel runs its own phone and email infrastructure, LC Phone and LC Email, billed through a prepaid Agency Wallet that auto-recharges your card when the balance runs low. The unit prices are small, the volumes are not. Per HighLevel's June 2026 billing guide:

  • Email: $0.675 per 1,000 sends on every plan. A 100,000-send month is about $67.50.
  • SMS: LC Phone matches Twilio pricing, roughly $0.0079 per segment in the US. Long texts split into multiple segments, so a 300-character blast counts two or three times.
  • Calls: about $0.014 per minute outbound and $0.0085 inbound in the US, plus a couple of dollars per month per phone number.
  • Premium workflow actions: $0.01 per execution for steps like webhooks and Slack notifications. Heavy automation users should grab a Workflow Pro tier: $10 for 10,000 executions, $25 for 30,000, or $50 for 65,000 per month.
  • Email validation: $2.50 per 1,000 checks, worth it to protect deliverability.
  • Common add-ons: dedicated sending IP $59 per month, WhatsApp $10 per month per sub-account plus usage, HIPAA compliance $297 per month (and note, once enabled it cannot be deactivated), white-label mobile app $497 per month, premium support $500 per month.

Realistic totals: a light single-business user adds $10 to $30 per month. A busy agency adds $70 to $150 or more. On the $297 and $497 plans you can rebill this usage to client sub-accounts, which is exactly what you should do from day one.

AI pricing in 2026: pay per use or $97 unlimited

HighLevel's AI suite is priced separately from the plans, with two billing modes.

Pay per use bills each AI product on its own meter. Conversation AI is now token-based at model API rates, for example GPT-5 at $1.25 per million input tokens and $10 per million output. Voice AI costs $0.06 per minute for the voice engine plus model tokens; HighLevel's own internal benchmark averages about $0.163 per minute, with normal phone-call charges on top. Reviews AI runs $0.01 per review response and Content AI about $0.09 per 1,000 words. A few things became free in 2026 under fair-use limits, including Funnel and Website AI, the Workflow AI builder, and Generate with AI.

AI Employee Unlimited is $97 per month per enabled sub-account and covers unlimited inbound Voice AI, Conversation AI, Reviews AI, Content AI, and Ask AI. Mind the exclusions: Agent Studio, the Voice AI widget, and outbound Voice AI calls still bill pay per use, and phone minutes always apply on voice calls. The per-sub-account part is the trap for agencies. Enable it on ten client accounts and that is $970 per month, so switch it on only where AI answers real volume, and rebill it at markup if you are on Agency Pro. Used that way, an AI receptionist that books even a handful of missed-call leads per month is one of the easier upsells in our AI automation work.

Three realistic monthly bills

  • Solo consultant, Starter: $97 plan, light SMS and email around $15, no paid AI. About $112 per month, replacing $200 or more in separate tools.
  • Agency with 10 clients, Unlimited: $297 plan, $100 to $200 of usage largely rebilled to clients at cost, AI Employee on three sub-accounts at $291 rebilled at $97 to $197 each. Net platform cost often lands under $350 while billing it onward.
  • SaaS reseller, Agency Pro: $497 plan, usage resold at markup, ten customers at $197 equals $1,970 MRR. Platform pays for itself several times over.

How to pay less

  • Go annual once you are sure: about 17 percent off, two months free.
  • Start on Starter, upgrade when client three arrives; upgrades are instant and prorated.
  • Turn on rebilling the day you hit Unlimited so usage flows to the sub-accounts that create it.
  • Buy a Workflow Pro tier before a big automation push; $0.01 per execution adds up fast.
  • Enable AI Employee per sub-account only where it answers real traffic, and review the wallet weekly under Agency Settings, then Billing.

Try GoHighLevel free for 14 days

Which plan should you pick in 2026?

Pick Starter at $97 if you are one business or an agency with up to three clients and no need for API access. Pick Unlimited at $297 if you run or plan to run an agency; it is the best value of the three and the plan most of our clients sit on. Pick Agency Pro at $497 only when you are committed to the SaaS reselling model, where markup rebilling turns the price into a margin engine.

The honest bottom line: GoHighLevel's sticker prices are fair for what consolidates into one login, but your real bill is plan plus usage plus AI, and the difference between a $120 month and a $500 month is configuration. If you want the platform set up to make money rather than burn wallet credit, our team builds, migrates, and runs GoHighLevel for agencies every week. Hire a GoHighLevel developer or grab the trial below and test it on one real campaign first.

faqs: How much does GoHighLevel cost in 2026?

GoHighLevel costs $97 per month for Starter, $297 per month for Unlimited, and $497 per month for Agency Pro. Annual billing cuts roughly 17 percent off each plan. On top of the subscription, usage is billed through a prepaid wallet: email at $0.675 per 1,000 sends, SMS at about $0.0079 per segment in the US, and optional AI either pay per use or $97 per month per sub-account.
What is the difference between the $97 and $297 GoHighLevel plans?

Starter at $97 includes the full platform but caps you at 3 sub-accounts and has no API access or rebilling. Unlimited at $297 removes the sub-account cap, adds API access and a branded desktop app, and lets you rebill SMS, email, and AI usage to clients at cost, which is why it is the standard agency choice.
Does GoHighLevel charge extra for SMS and email?

Yes. Texts and emails are billed through the Agency Wallet on every plan. Email costs $0.675 per 1,000 sends, SMS runs about $0.0079 per segment in the US at Twilio-parity rates, and outbound calls are about $0.014 per minute. Light users spend $10 to $30 per month on usage, busy agencies $70 to $150 or more, and plans from $297 up can pass these costs to client sub-accounts.
How much does GoHighLevel AI cost?

Two options in 2026: pay per use, where Conversation AI bills tokens at model API rates, Voice AI averages about $0.163 per minute, and Reviews AI is $0.01 per response; or AI Employee Unlimited at $97 per month per enabled sub-account, covering unlimited inbound Voice AI, Conversation AI, Reviews AI, Content AI, and Ask AI. Agent Studio, the Voice AI widget, and outbound Voice AI calls stay pay per use.
Is there a GoHighLevel free trial?

Yes, GoHighLevel offers a 14-day free trial on all three plans. You enter a card up front and can cancel inside the trial window without being charged. It is enough time to migrate one pipeline, build a funnel, and test the AI tools on real traffic before committing.
Is GoHighLevel worth $297 a month for an agency?

For most agencies, yes. The $297 Unlimited plan replaces separate tools for CRM, funnels, email, scheduling, call tracking, and reputation management, and the subscription stays flat no matter how many client sub-accounts you add. Two to three retained clients typically cover the cost. The main caveats are the learning curve and usage fees, which you should rebill to clients from day one.
